The name Amatsubaru-no-Mikoto-no-Mitama is derived from "Subaru" (Pleiades), and it is believed to have been associated with agricultural divination based on celestial observation.<ref name="ic"/> Hayakawa-hiko-no-Mikoto and Hayakawa-hime-no-Mikoto, enshrined at {{ill|Satakunari Shrine|ja|狭田国生神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Satakunari Shrine|qq=}}, are considered the children of Amatsubaru-no-Mikoto-no-Mitama.<ref name="th">Kaneko (1984): p.59</ref> Additionally, Nagakuchi-me-no-Mikoto, another child, is enshrined at {{ill|E Shrine|ja|狭田国生神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/E Shrine|qq=}}.<ref>Shikinaisha Kenkyukai (1990): p.306</ref> There is no physical sacred object representing the deity.<ref name="th"/>
== History == Sugihara Shrine is the newest among the auxiliary shrines in Tamaki Town, established during the Nara period after the Arakida clan developed the surrounding area.<ref>Kaneko (1984): p.69</ref> Initially, Mimae Shrine stood in front of Sugihara Shrine.<ref name="tm"/>
The shrine was abandoned during the Sengoku period but was restored on September 8, 1663 (Gregorian calendar: October 8, 1663) through the efforts of {{ill|Sakatekunari Shrine|ja|坂手国生神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sakatekunari Shrine|qq=}} and Shinto priests led by Kawabe Masanaga.<ref>Shikinaisha Kenkyukai (1990): p.277</ref> During its restoration, Mimae Shrine was merged into Sugihara Shrine.<ref name="tm"/> The restoration process was challenging as the exact location of the original shrine had been lost.<ref name="sk8 P359">Shikinaisha Kenkyukai (1990): p.359</ref>
The shrine was rebuilt in August 1918.<ref name="uy">Ujiyamada City Office (1929): p.11</ref>
== Rituals == Rituals are conducted by Shinto priests during major festivals, including the Three Seasonal Festivals (Tsukinamisai, Kannamesai), the Prayer Festival (Kinen-sai), the Harvest Festival (Niinamesai), and temporary offering festivals (Rinjiheihai-sai).<ref name="sk1"/>
== Surroundings == Rice paddies spread out to the south of the shrine grounds.<ref name="ic"/> On clear days, the Ise Sanzan (Three Mountains of Ise) can be seen to the west.<ref name="ic"/>
* **By Train:** Approximately 13 minutes on foot (about 715 m) from JR Sangu Line's Sotoda Station. * **By Car:** About 10 minutes (approximately 5.0 km) from the Tamaki Interchange on the Ise Expressway. == References ==

'''{{ill|Sugihara Shrine|ja|棒原神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sugihara Shrine|qq=}}''' (*Sugihara Jinja*) is an auxiliary shrine (*sessha*) of {{ill|Kōtai Jingū|ja|皇大神宮|1=Kōtai Jingū|2=ja|3=皇大神宮|4=zh|5=皇大神宮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kōtai Jingū|13=User:Immanuelle/Kōtai Jingū|comment=enwiki is a redirect}} (the Inner Shrine of the [[Ise Grand Shrine]]), located in [[Tamaki Town]], [[:en:Mie Prefecture|Mie Prefecture]], Japan. It Ranks 23rd among the auxiliary shrines of the Inner Shrine.<ref>Ujiyamada City Office (1929): pp. 9–11.</ref>
The shrine is dedicated to a deity associated with the [[:en:Pleiades|Pleiades]] (*Subaru*), suggesting that ancient star-based divination practices were performed in this region.<ref name="ic">Ise Bunka Publishing (2008): p. 66.</ref><ref name="gk">Gakken Publishing (2013): p. 67.</ref>
== Overview == [[File:Sugihara-jinja 01.jpg|left|thumb|upright|Stone Steps Leading to the Shrine]]
Sugihara Shrine (杉原神社) is located in Asakuda 2466, Kamitanui, Tamaki Town, Watarai District, Mie Prefecture.<ref name="tm">Tatsumi Publishing (2013): p.57</ref> It is one of the 13 auxiliary shrines of the Inner Shrine in Tamaki Town. The shrine is situated atop a hill locally referred to as "Sugi no Mori" (Cedar Grove), with the shrine building located at the top of an 88-step stone staircase.<ref name="gk"/>
While the official reading of the shrine's name by Ise Grand Shrine is "Sugihara," some manuscripts of the "{{ill|Engishiki Jinmyōchō|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|ja|延喜式神名庫|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Engishiki Jinmyōchō|13=User:Immanuelle/Engishiki Jinmyōchō|qq=}}" read it as "Oihara."<ref name="sk8">Shikinaisha Kenkyukai (1990): p.358</ref>
The shrine precinct covers approximately 17,078 m², and the shrine building is constructed in the Shinmei-zukuri style with wooden plank roofing.<ref name="sk1">Shikinaisha Kenkyukai (1990): p.361</ref> The site includes a tamagaki (sacred fence) and a torii gate.<ref name="sk1"/> In ancient times, the shrine grounds spanned about 29,751 m², featuring two shrine buildings each measuring approximately 1.8 m x 1.2 m x 2.1 m.<ref name="sk8"/> The grounds also contain the "Asakuda Kofun Group," a collection of small Kofuns, including 10 round kofun and 14 square kofun.<ref name="sk1"/>

The name Amatsubaru-no-Mikoto-no-Mitama is derived from "Subaru" (Pleiades), and it is believed to have been associated with agricultural divination based on celestial observation.<ref name="ic"/> Hayakawa-hiko-no-Mikoto and Hayakawa-hime-no-Mikoto, enshrined at {{ill|Satakunari Shrine|ja|狭田国生神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Satakunari Shrine|qq=}}, are considered the children of Amatsubaru-no-Mikoto-no-Mitama.<ref name="th">Kaneko (1984): p.59</ref> Additionally, Nagakuchi-me-no-Mikoto, another child, is enshrined at {{ill|E Shrine|ja|狭田国生神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/E Shrine|qq=}}.<ref>Shikinaisha Kenkyukai (1990): p.306</ref> There is no physical sacred object representing the deity.<ref name="th"/>
== History == Sugihara Shrine is the newest among the auxiliary shrines in Tamaki Town, established during the Nara period after the Arakida clan developed the surrounding area.<ref>Kaneko (1984): p.69</ref> Initially, Mimae Shrine stood in front of Sugihara Shrine.<ref name="tm"/>
The shrine was abandoned during the Sengoku period but was restored on September 8, 1663 (Gregorian calendar: October 8, 1663) through the efforts of {{ill|Sakatekunari Shrine|ja|坂手国生神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sakatekunari Shrine|qq=}} and Shinto priests led by Kawabe Masanaga.<ref>Shikinaisha Kenkyukai (1990): p.277</ref> During its restoration, Mimae Shrine was merged into Sugihara Shrine.<ref name="tm"/> The restoration process was challenging as the exact location of the original shrine had been lost.<ref name="sk8 P359">Shikinaisha Kenkyukai (1990): p.359</ref>
The shrine was rebuilt in August 1918.<ref name="uy">Ujiyamada City Office (1929): p.11</ref>
== Rituals == Rituals are conducted by Shinto priests during major festivals, including the Three Seasonal Festivals (Tsukinamisai, Kannamesai), the Prayer Festival (Kinen-sai), the Harvest Festival (Niinamesai), and temporary offering festivals (Rinjiheihai-sai).<ref name="sk1"/>
== Surroundings == Rice paddies spread out to the south of the shrine grounds.<ref name="ic"/> On clear days, the Ise Sanzan (Three Mountains of Ise) can be seen to the west.<ref name="ic"/>
* **By Train:** Approximately 13 minutes on foot (about 715 m) from JR Sangu Line's Sotoda Station. * **By Car:** About 10 minutes (approximately 5.0 km) from the Tamaki Interchange on the Ise Expressway. == References ==

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:棒原神社]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:棒原神社]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:棒原神社]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q17218865]] Wikidata name: [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:33, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Infobox religious building| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | Name = {{ill|Sugihara Shrine|ja|棒原神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sugihara Shrine|qq=}} | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | image = File:Sugihara-jinja 02.jpg | location = Kamitanabe, Tamaki Town, [[:en:Watarai District|Watarai District]], [[:en:Mie Prefecture|Mie Prefecture]], Japan | coordinates = {{Coord|34.498833|N|136.60325|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | Map = Japan Mie | deity = Amatsusubarume-no-Mikoto (*天須婆留女命御魂*), Omakami (*御前神*) | type = {{ill|Engishiki Jinmyōchō|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|ja|延喜式神名帳|lt=Shikinaisha}} (minor Rank), {{ill|Kōtai Jingū|ja|皇大神宮|1=Kōtai Jingū|2=ja|3=皇大神宮|4=zh|5=皇大神宮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kōtai Jingū|13=User:Immanuelle/Kōtai Jingū|comment=enwiki is a redirect}} auxiliary shrine (*sessha*) | Established = [[Nara period]] | Main Hall = [[Shinmei-zukuri]] | Alternate Name = Sugino Mori (*杉の森*) | festival = [[Kannamesai Festival|Kannamesai]] (*神嘗祭*) | location = {{ill|Tamaki|WD=Q587348}} | architecture_style = {{ill|Shinmei-zukuri|WD=Q930545}} }}
'''{{ill|Sugihara Shrine|ja|棒原神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sugihara Shrine|qq=}}''' (*Sugihara Jinja*) is an auxiliary shrine (*sessha*) of {{ill|Kōtai Jingū|ja|皇大神宮|1=Kōtai Jingū|2=ja|3=皇大神宮|4=zh|5=皇大神宮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kōtai Jingū|13=User:Immanuelle/Kōtai Jingū|comment=enwiki is a redirect}} (the Inner Shrine of the [[Ise Grand Shrine]]), located in [[Tamaki Town]], [[:en:Mie Prefecture|Mie Prefecture]], Japan. It Ranks 23rd among the auxiliary shrines of the Inner Shrine.<ref>Ujiyamada City Office (1929): pp. 9–11.</ref>
The shrine is dedicated to a deity associated with the [[:en:Pleiades|Pleiades]] (*Subaru*), suggesting that ancient star-based divination practices were performed in this region.<ref name="ic">Ise Bunka Publishing (2008): p. 66.</ref><ref name="gk">Gakken Publishing (2013): p. 67.</ref>
== Overview == [[File:Sugihara-jinja 01.jpg|left|thumb|upright|Stone Steps Leading to the Shrine]]
Sugihara Shrine (杉原神社) is located in Asakuda 2466, Kamitanui, Tamaki Town, Watarai District, Mie Prefecture.<ref name="tm">Tatsumi Publishing (2013): p.57</ref> It is one of the 13 auxiliary shrines of the Inner Shrine in Tamaki Town. The shrine is situated atop a hill locally referred to as "Sugi no Mori" (Cedar Grove), with the shrine building located at the top of an 88-step stone staircase.<ref name="gk"/>
While the official reading of the shrine's name by Ise Grand Shrine is "Sugihara," some manuscripts of the "{{ill|Engishiki Jinmyōchō|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|ja|延喜式神名庫|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Engishiki Jinmyōchō|13=User:Immanuelle/Engishiki Jinmyōchō|qq=}}" read it as "Oihara."<ref name="sk8">Shikinaisha Kenkyukai (1990): p.358</ref>
The shrine precinct covers approximately 17,078 m², and the shrine building is constructed in the Shinmei-zukuri style with wooden plank roofing.<ref name="sk1">Shikinaisha Kenkyukai (1990): p.361</ref> The site includes a tamagaki (sacred fence) and a torii gate.<ref name="sk1"/> In ancient times, the shrine grounds spanned about 29,751 m², featuring two shrine buildings each measuring approximately 1.8 m x 1.2 m x 2.1 m.<ref name="sk8"/> The grounds also contain the "Asakuda Kofun Group," a collection of small Kofuns, including 10 round kofun and 14 square kofun.<ref name="sk1"/>
== Enshrined Deities == The enshrined deities are Amatsubaru-no-Mikoto-no-Mitama (天須婆留女命御魂) and Mimae-no-Kami (御前神).<ref name="ic"/><ref name="gk"/> They are considered guardian deities of the surrounding hills.<ref name="ic"/>
The name Amatsubaru-no-Mikoto-no-Mitama is derived from "Subaru" (Pleiades), and it is believed to have been associated with agricultural divination based on celestial observation.<ref name="ic"/> Hayakawa-hiko-no-Mikoto and Hayakawa-hime-no-Mikoto, enshrined at {{ill|Satakunari Shrine|ja|狭田国生神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Satakunari Shrine|qq=}}, are considered the children of Amatsubaru-no-Mikoto-no-Mitama.<ref name="th">Kaneko (1984): p.59</ref> Additionally, Nagakuchi-me-no-Mikoto, another child, is enshrined at {{ill|E Shrine|ja|狭田国生神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/E Shrine|qq=}}.<ref>Shikinaisha Kenkyukai (1990): p.306</ref> There is no physical sacred object representing the deity.<ref name="th"/>
== History == Sugihara Shrine is the newest among the auxiliary shrines in Tamaki Town, established during the Nara period after the Arakida clan developed the surrounding area.<ref>Kaneko (1984): p.69</ref> Initially, Mimae Shrine stood in front of Sugihara Shrine.<ref name="tm"/>
The shrine was abandoned during the Sengoku period but was restored on September 8, 1663 (Gregorian calendar: October 8, 1663) through the efforts of {{ill|Sakatekunari Shrine|ja|坂手国生神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sakatekunari Shrine|qq=}} and Shinto priests led by Kawabe Masanaga.<ref>Shikinaisha Kenkyukai (1990): p.277</ref> During its restoration, Mimae Shrine was merged into Sugihara Shrine.<ref name="tm"/> The restoration process was challenging as the exact location of the original shrine had been lost.<ref name="sk8 P359">Shikinaisha Kenkyukai (1990): p.359</ref>
The shrine was rebuilt in August 1918.<ref name="uy">Ujiyamada City Office (1929): p.11</ref>
== Rituals == Rituals are conducted by Shinto priests during major festivals, including the Three Seasonal Festivals (Tsukinamisai, Kannamesai), the Prayer Festival (Kinen-sai), the Harvest Festival (Niinamesai), and temporary offering festivals (Rinjiheihai-sai).<ref name="sk1"/>
== Surroundings == Rice paddies spread out to the south of the shrine grounds.<ref name="ic"/> On clear days, the Ise Sanzan (Three Mountains of Ise) can be seen to the west.<ref name="ic"/>
* **By Train:** Approximately 13 minutes on foot (about 715 m) from JR Sangu Line's Sotoda Station. * **By Car:** About 10 minutes (approximately 5.0 km) from the Tamaki Interchange on the Ise Expressway. == References ==

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:棒原神社]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:棒原神社]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:棒原神社]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q17218865]] Wikidata name: [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:33, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Infobox religious building| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | Name = {{ill|Sugihara Shrine|ja|棒原神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sugihara Shrine|qq=}} | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | image = File:Sugihara-jinja 02.jpg | location = Kamitanabe, Tamaki Town, [[:en:Watarai District|Watarai District]], [[:en:Mie Prefecture|Mie Prefecture]], Japan | coordinates = {{Coord|34.498833|N|136.60325|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | Map = Japan Mie | deity = Amatsusubarume-no-Mikoto (*天須婆留女命御魂*), Omakami (*御前神*) | type = {{ill|Engishiki Jinmyōchō|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|ja|延喜式神名帳|lt=Shikinaisha}} (minor Rank), {{ill|Kōtai Jingū|ja|皇大神宮|1=Kōtai Jingū|2=ja|3=皇大神宮|4=zh|5=皇大神宮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kōtai Jingū|13=User:Immanuelle/Kōtai Jingū|comment=enwiki is a redirect}} auxiliary shrine (*sessha*) | Established = [[Nara period]] | Main Hall = [[Shinmei-zukuri]] | Alternate Name = Sugino Mori (*杉の森*) | festival = [[Kannamesai Festival|Kannamesai]] (*神嘗祭*) | location = {{ill|Tamaki|WD=Q587348}} | architecture_style = {{ill|Shinmei-zukuri|WD=Q930545}} }}
'''{{ill|Sugihara Shrine|ja|棒原神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sugihara Shrine|qq=}}''' (*Sugihara Jinja*) is an auxiliary shrine (*sessha*) of {{ill|Kōtai Jingū|ja|皇大神宮|1=Kōtai Jingū|2=ja|3=皇大神宮|4=zh|5=皇大神宮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kōtai Jingū|13=User:Immanuelle/Kōtai Jingū|comment=enwiki is a redirect}} (the Inner Shrine of the [[Ise Grand Shrine]]), located in [[Tamaki Town]], [[:en:Mie Prefecture|Mie Prefecture]], Japan. It Ranks 23rd among the auxiliary shrines of the Inner Shrine.<ref>Ujiyamada City Office (1929): pp. 9–11.</ref>
The shrine is dedicated to a deity associated with the [[:en:Pleiades|Pleiades]] (*Subaru*), suggesting that ancient star-based divination practices were performed in this region.<ref name="ic">Ise Bunka Publishing (2008): p. 66.</ref><ref name="gk">Gakken Publishing (2013): p. 67.</ref>
== Overview == [[File:Sugihara-jinja 01.jpg|left|thumb|upright|Stone Steps Leading to the Shrine]]
Sugihara Shrine (杉原神社) is located in Asakuda 2466, Kamitanui, Tamaki Town, Watarai District, Mie Prefecture.<ref name="tm">Tatsumi Publishing (2013): p.57</ref> It is one of the 13 auxiliary shrines of the Inner Shrine in Tamaki Town. The shrine is situated atop a hill locally referred to as "Sugi no Mori" (Cedar Grove), with the shrine building located at the top of an 88-step stone staircase.<ref name="gk"/>
While the official reading of the shrine's name by Ise Grand Shrine is "Sugihara," some manuscripts of the "{{ill|Engishiki Jinmyōchō|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|ja|延喜式神名庫|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Engishiki Jinmyōchō|13=User:Immanuelle/Engishiki Jinmyōchō|qq=}}" read it as "Oihara."<ref name="sk8">Shikinaisha Kenkyukai (1990): p.358</ref>
The shrine precinct covers approximately 17,078 m², and the shrine building is constructed in the Shinmei-zukuri style with wooden plank roofing.<ref name="sk1">Shikinaisha Kenkyukai (1990): p.361</ref> The site includes a tamagaki (sacred fence) and a torii gate.<ref name="sk1"/> In ancient times, the shrine grounds spanned about 29,751 m², featuring two shrine buildings each measuring approximately 1.8 m x 1.2 m x 2.1 m.<ref name="sk8"/> The grounds also contain the "Asakuda Kofun Group," a collection of small Kofuns, including 10 round kofun and 14 square kofun.<ref name="sk1"/>
== Enshrined Deities == The enshrined deities are Amatsubaru-no-Mikoto-no-Mitama (天須婆留女命御魂) and Mimae-no-Kami (御前神).<ref name="ic"/><ref name="gk"/> They are considered guardian deities of the surrounding hills.<ref name="ic"/>
The name Amatsubaru-no-Mikoto-no-Mitama is derived from "Subaru" (Pleiades), and it is believed to have been associated with agricultural divination based on celestial observation.<ref name="ic"/> Hayakawa-hiko-no-Mikoto and Hayakawa-hime-no-Mikoto, enshrined at {{ill|Satakunari Shrine|ja|狭田国生神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Satakunari Shrine|qq=}}, are considered the children of Amatsubaru-no-Mikoto-no-Mitama.<ref name="th">Kaneko (1984): p.59</ref> Additionally, Nagakuchi-me-no-Mikoto, another child, is enshrined at {{ill|E Shrine|ja|狭田国生神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/E Shrine|qq=}}.<ref>Shikinaisha Kenkyukai (1990): p.306</ref> There is no physical sacred object representing the deity.<ref name="th"/>
== History == Sugihara Shrine is the newest among the auxiliary shrines in Tamaki Town, established during the Nara period after the Arakida clan developed the surrounding area.<ref>Kaneko (1984): p.69</ref> Initially, Mimae Shrine stood in front of Sugihara Shrine.<ref name="tm"/>
The shrine was abandoned during the Sengoku period but was restored on September 8, 1663 (Gregorian calendar: October 8, 1663) through the efforts of {{ill|Sakatekunari Shrine|ja|坂手国生神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sakatekunari Shrine|qq=}} and Shinto priests led by Kawabe Masanaga.<ref>Shikinaisha Kenkyukai (1990): p.277</ref> During its restoration, Mimae Shrine was merged into Sugihara Shrine.<ref name="tm"/> The restoration process was challenging as the exact location of the original shrine had been lost.<ref name="sk8 P359">Shikinaisha Kenkyukai (1990): p.359</ref>
The shrine was rebuilt in August 1918.<ref name="uy">Ujiyamada City Office (1929): p.11</ref>
== Rituals == Rituals are conducted by Shinto priests during major festivals, including the Three Seasonal Festivals (Tsukinamisai, Kannamesai), the Prayer Festival (Kinen-sai), the Harvest Festival (Niinamesai), and temporary offering festivals (Rinjiheihai-sai).<ref name="sk1"/>
== Surroundings == Rice paddies spread out to the south of the shrine grounds.<ref name="ic"/> On clear days, the Ise Sanzan (Three Mountains of Ise) can be seen to the west.<ref name="ic"/>
* **By Train:** Approximately 13 minutes on foot (about 715 m) from JR Sangu Line's Sotoda Station. * **By Car:** About 10 minutes (approximately 5.0 km) from the Tamaki Interchange on the Ise Expressway. == References ==

ISBN 978-4-900759-37-4 * 宇治山田市役所 編『宇治山田市史 上巻』宇治山田市役所、昭和4th year 1月20日、862p. * [[:en:学研パブリッシング|学研パブリッシング]]『伊勢神宮に行こう』Gakken Mook神社紀行セレクションvol.1、[[薗田稔]]監修、学研マーケティング、2013rd year 7月4日、82p. <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:棒原神社]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:棒原神社]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:棒原神社]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q17218865]] Wikidata name: [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:33, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Infobox religious building| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | Name = {{ill|Sugihara Shrine|ja|棒原神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sugihara Shrine|qq=}} | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | image = File:Sugihara-jinja 02.jpg | location = Kamitanabe, Tamaki Town, {{ill|Watarai District|en|Watarai District|WD=Q1131032|lt=Watarai District}}, {{ill|Mie Prefecture|en|Mie Prefecture|WD=Q128196|lt=Mie Prefecture}}, Japan | coordinates = {{Coord|34.498833|N|136.60325|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | Map = Japan Mie | deity = Amatsusubarume-no-Mikoto (*天須婆留女命御魂*), Omakami (*御前神*) | type = {{ill|Engishiki Jinmyōchō|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|ja|延喜式神名帳|lt=Shikinaisha}} (minor Rank), {{ill|Kōtai Jingū|ja|皇大神宮|1=Kōtai Jingū|2=ja|3=皇大神宮|4=zh|5=皇大神宮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kōtai Jingū|13=User:Immanuelle/Kōtai Jingū|comment=enwiki is a redirect}} auxiliary shrine (*sessha*) | Established = [[Nara period]] | Main Hall = [[Shinmei-zukuri]] | Alternate Name = Sugino Mori (*杉の森*) | festival = [[Kannamesai Festival|Kannamesai]] (*神嘗祭*) | location = {{ill|Tamaki|WD=Q587348}} | architecture_style = {{ill|Shinmei-zukuri|WD=Q930545}} }}
'''{{ill|Sugihara Shrine|ja|棒原神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sugihara Shrine|qq=}}''' (*Sugihara Jinja*) is an auxiliary shrine (*sessha*) of {{ill|Kōtai Jingū|ja|皇大神宮|1=Kōtai Jingū|2=ja|3=皇大神宮|4=zh|5=皇大神宮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kōtai Jingū|13=User:Immanuelle/Kōtai Jingū|comment=enwiki is a redirect}} (the Inner Shrine of the [[Ise Grand Shrine]]), located in [[Tamaki Town]], {{ill|Mie Prefecture|en|Mie Prefecture|WD=Q128196|lt=Mie Prefecture}}, Japan. It Ranks 23rd among the auxiliary shrines of the Inner Shrine.<ref>Ujiyamada City Office (1929): pp. 9–11.</ref>
The shrine is dedicated to a deity associated with the {{ill|Pleiades|en|Pleiades|WD=Q13423|lt=Pleiades}} (*Subaru*), suggesting that ancient star-based divination practices were performed in this region.<ref name="ic">Ise Bunka Publishing (2008): p. 66.</ref><ref name="gk">Gakken Publishing (2013): p. 67.</ref>
== Overview == [[File:Sugihara-jinja 01.jpg|left|thumb|upright|Stone Steps Leading to the Shrine]]
Sugihara Shrine (杉原神社) is located in Asakuda 2466, Kamitanui, Tamaki Town, Watarai District, Mie Prefecture.<ref name="tm">Tatsumi Publishing (2013): p.57</ref> It is one of the 13 auxiliary shrines of the Inner Shrine in Tamaki Town. The shrine is situated atop a hill locally referred to as "Sugi no Mori" (Cedar Grove), with the shrine building located at the top of an 88-step stone staircase.<ref name="gk"/>
While the official reading of the shrine's name by Ise Grand Shrine is "Sugihara," some manuscripts of the "{{ill|Engishiki Jinmyōchō|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|ja|延喜式神名庫|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Engishiki Jinmyōchō|13=User:Immanuelle/Engishiki Jinmyōchō|qq=}}" read it as "Oihara."<ref name="sk8">Shikinaisha Kenkyukai (1990): p.358</ref>
The shrine precinct covers approximately 17,078 m², and the shrine building is constructed in the Shinmei-zukuri style with wooden plank roofing.<ref name="sk1">Shikinaisha Kenkyukai (1990): p.361</ref> The site includes a tamagaki (sacred fence) and a torii gate.<ref name="sk1"/> In ancient times, the shrine grounds spanned about 29,751 m², featuring two shrine buildings each measuring approximately 1.8 m x 1.2 m x 2.1 m.<ref name="sk8"/> The grounds also contain the "Asakuda Kofun Group," a collection of small Kofuns, including 10 round kofun and 14 square kofun.<ref name="sk1"/>
== Enshrined Deities == The enshrined deities are Amatsubaru-no-Mikoto-no-Mitama (天須婆留女命御魂) and Mimae-no-Kami (御前神).<ref name="ic"/><ref name="gk"/> They are considered guardian deities of the surrounding hills.<ref name="ic"/>
The name Amatsubaru-no-Mikoto-no-Mitama is derived from "Subaru" (Pleiades), and it is believed to have been associated with agricultural divination based on celestial observation.<ref name="ic"/> Hayakawa-hiko-no-Mikoto and Hayakawa-hime-no-Mikoto, enshrined at {{ill|Satakunari Shrine|ja|狭田国生神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Satakunari Shrine|qq=}}, are considered the children of Amatsubaru-no-Mikoto-no-Mitama.<ref name="th">Kaneko (1984): p.59</ref> Additionally, Nagakuchi-me-no-Mikoto, another child, is enshrined at {{ill|E Shrine|ja|狭田国生神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/E Shrine|qq=}}.<ref>Shikinaisha Kenkyukai (1990): p.306</ref> There is no physical sacred object representing the deity.<ref name="th"/>
== History == Sugihara Shrine is the newest among the auxiliary shrines in Tamaki Town, established during the Nara period after the Arakida clan developed the surrounding area.<ref>Kaneko (1984): p.69</ref> Initially, Mimae Shrine stood in front of Sugihara Shrine.<ref name="tm"/>
The shrine was abandoned during the Sengoku period but was restored on September 8, 1663 (Gregorian calendar: October 8, 1663) through the efforts of {{ill|Sakatekunari Shrine|ja|坂手国生神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sakatekunari Shrine|qq=}} and Shinto priests led by Kawabe Masanaga.<ref>Shikinaisha Kenkyukai (1990): p.277</ref> During its restoration, Mimae Shrine was merged into Sugihara Shrine.<ref name="tm"/> The restoration process was challenging as the exact location of the original shrine had been lost.<ref name="sk8 P359">Shikinaisha Kenkyukai (1990): p.359</ref>
The shrine was rebuilt in August 1918.<ref name="uy">Ujiyamada City Office (1929): p.11</ref>
== Rituals == Rituals are conducted by Shinto priests during major festivals, including the Three Seasonal Festivals (Tsukinamisai, Kannamesai), the Prayer Festival (Kinen-sai), the Harvest Festival (Niinamesai), and temporary offering festivals (Rinjiheihai-sai).<ref name="sk1"/>
== Surroundings == Rice paddies spread out to the south of the shrine grounds.<ref name="ic"/> On clear days, the Ise Sanzan (Three Mountains of Ise) can be seen to the west.<ref name="ic"/>
* **By Train:** Approximately 13 minutes on foot (about 715 m) from JR Sangu Line's Sotoda Station. * **By Car:** About 10 minutes (approximately 5.0 km) from the Tamaki Interchange on the Ise Expressway. == References ==
{{Reflist|2}}
== Bibliography == * [[伊勢文化舎]] 編『お伊勢さん125社めぐり』別冊『伊勢人』、伊勢文化舎、平成20th year 12月23日、151p. ISBN 978-4-900759-37-4 * 宇治山田市役所 編『宇治山田市史 上巻』宇治山田市役所、昭和4th year 1月20日、862p. * [[:en:学研パブリッシング|学研パブリッシング]]『伊勢神宮に行こう』Gakken Mook神社紀行セレクションvol.1、[[薗田稔]]監修、学研マーケティング、2013rd year 7月4日、82p. ISBN 978-4-05-610047-1 * 金子延夫『玉城町史 第3巻―南伊勢の歴史と伝承―』三重県郷土資料叢書第92集、三重県郷土史料刊行会、昭和59th year 3月10日、308p. * 式内社研究会 編『式内社調査報告 第六巻 東海道1』{{ill|Kogakkan University|en|Kogakkan University|WD=Q1797584|lt=Kogakkan University}}出版部、平成2nd year 2月28日、690p. ISBN 4-87644-080-8 * [[福山敏男]]・[[稲垣栄三|稲垣榮三]]・村瀬美樹・胡麻鶴醇之『神宮―第六十回神宮式年遷宮―』[[:en:小学館|小学館]]、昭和50th year 4月20日、246p. * 『日本人なら知っておきたい 伊勢神宮と125の社』タツミムック、[[辰巳出版]]、2013rd year 11月1日、127p. ISBN 978-4-7778-1197-7 == See Also == * {{ill|List of 125 Shrines of Ise|ja|神宮125社の一覧|lt=List of 125 Shrines of Ise|1=List of Shikinaisha in Ise Province|3=伊勢国の式内社一覧|1=List of Shikinaisha in Ise Province|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/List of 125 Shrines of Ise|13=User:Immanuelle/List of Shikinaisha in Ise Province|qq=}} * [[田丸町]]
== External Links == {{ external media | align = | width = 230px | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image1 = [http://www.sengukan.jp/wp-content/themes/sengukan/images/shrine/area03/area03_05.jpg 棒原神社] }} * [http://www.jingukaikan.jp/125mairi/m07/0707.html 棒原神社（すぎはらじんじゃ）] - 財団法人伊勢神宮崇敬会 * [http://www.town.tamaki.mie.jp/tamnet/sankan/jinjya/ 産業観光神社めぐり] - 三重県玉城町 {{神道 横}}

'''{{ill|Sugihara Shrine|ja|棒原神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sugihara Shrine|qq=}}''' (*Sugihara Jinja*) is an auxiliary shrine (*sessha*) of {{ill|Kōtai Jingū|ja|皇大神宮|1=Kōtai Jingū|2=ja|3=皇大神宮|4=zh|5=皇大神宮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kōtai Jingū|13=User:Immanuelle/Kōtai Jingū|comment=enwiki is a redirect}} (the Inner Shrine of the [[Ise Grand Shrine]]), located in [[Tamaki Town]], {{ill|Mie Prefecture|en|Mie Prefecture|WD=Q128196|lt=Mie Prefecture}}, Japan. It Ranks 23rd among the auxiliary shrines of the Inner Shrine.<ref>Ujiyamada City Office (1929): pp. 9–11.</ref>
The shrine is dedicated to a deity associated with the {{ill|Pleiades|en|Pleiades|WD=Q13423|lt=Pleiades}} (*Subaru*), suggesting that ancient star-based divination practices were performed in this region.<ref name="ic">Ise Bunka Publishing (2008): p. 66.</ref><ref name="gk">Gakken Publishing (2013): p. 67.</ref>
== Overview == [[File:Sugihara-jinja 01.jpg|left|thumb|upright|Stone Steps Leading to the Shrine]]
Sugihara Shrine (杉原神社) is located in Asakuda 2466, Kamitanui, Tamaki Town, Watarai District, Mie Prefecture.<ref name="tm">Tatsumi Publishing (2013): p.57</ref> It is one of the 13 auxiliary shrines of the Inner Shrine in Tamaki Town. The shrine is situated atop a hill locally referred to as "Sugi no Mori" (Cedar Grove), with the shrine building located at the top of an 88-step stone staircase.<ref name="gk"/>
While the official reading of the shrine's name by Ise Grand Shrine is "Sugihara," some manuscripts of the "{{ill|Engishiki Jinmyōchō|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|ja|延喜式神名庫|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Engishiki Jinmyōchō|13=User:Immanuelle/Engishiki Jinmyōchō|qq=}}" read it as "Oihara."<ref name="sk8">Shikinaisha Kenkyukai (1990): p.358</ref>
The shrine precinct covers approximately 17,078 m², and the shrine building is constructed in the Shinmei-zukuri style with wooden plank roofing.<ref name="sk1">Shikinaisha Kenkyukai (1990): p.361</ref> The site includes a tamagaki (sacred fence) and a torii gate.<ref name="sk1"/> In ancient times, the shrine grounds spanned about 29,751 m², featuring two shrine buildings each measuring approximately 1.8 m x 1.2 m x 2.1 m.<ref name="sk8"/> The grounds also contain the "Asakuda Kofun Group," a collection of small Kofuns, including 10 round kofun and 14 square kofun.<ref name="sk1"/>
== Enshrined Deities == The enshrined deities are Amatsubaru-no-Mikoto-no-Mitama (天須婆留女命御魂) and Mimae-no-Kami (御前神).<ref name="ic"/><ref name="gk"/> They are considered guardian deities of the surrounding hills.<ref name="ic"/>
The name Amatsubaru-no-Mikoto-no-Mitama is derived from "Subaru" (Pleiades), and it is believed to have been associated with agricultural divination based on celestial observation.<ref name="ic"/> Hayakawa-hiko-no-Mikoto and Hayakawa-hime-no-Mikoto, enshrined at {{ill|Satakunari Shrine|ja|狭田国生神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Satakunari Shrine|qq=}}, are considered the children of Amatsubaru-no-Mikoto-no-Mitama.<ref name="th">Kaneko (1984): p.59</ref> Additionally, Nagakuchi-me-no-Mikoto, another child, is enshrined at {{ill|E Shrine|ja|狭田国生神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/E Shrine|qq=}}.<ref>Shikinaisha Kenkyukai (1990): p.306</ref> There is no physical sacred object representing the deity.<ref name="th"/>
== History == Sugihara Shrine is the newest among the auxiliary shrines in Tamaki Town, established during the Nara period after the Arakida clan developed the surrounding area.<ref>Kaneko (1984): p.69</ref> Initially, Mimae Shrine stood in front of Sugihara Shrine.<ref name="tm"/>
The shrine was abandoned during the Sengoku period but was restored on September 8, 1663 (Gregorian calendar: October 8, 1663) through the efforts of {{ill|Sakatekunari Shrine|ja|坂手国生神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sakatekunari Shrine|qq=}} and Shinto priests led by Kawabe Masanaga.<ref>Shikinaisha Kenkyukai (1990): p.277</ref> During its restoration, Mimae Shrine was merged into Sugihara Shrine.<ref name="tm"/> The restoration process was challenging as the exact location of the original shrine had been lost.<ref name="sk8 P359">Shikinaisha Kenkyukai (1990): p.359</ref>
The shrine was rebuilt in August 1918.<ref name="uy">Ujiyamada City Office (1929): p.11</ref>
== Rituals == Rituals are conducted by Shinto priests during major festivals, including the Three Seasonal Festivals (Tsukinamisai, Kannamesai), the Prayer Festival (Kinen-sai), the Harvest Festival (Niinamesai), and temporary offering festivals (Rinjiheihai-sai).<ref name="sk1"/>
== Surroundings == Rice paddies spread out to the south of the shrine grounds.<ref name="ic"/> On clear days, the Ise Sanzan (Three Mountains of Ise) can be seen to the west.<ref name="ic"/>
* **By Train:** Approximately 13 minutes on foot (about 715 m) from JR Sangu Line's Sotoda Station. * **By Car:** About 10 minutes (approximately 5.0 km) from the Tamaki Interchange on the Ise Expressway. == References ==
{{Reflist|2}}

ISBN 4-87644-080-8 * [[福山敏男]]・[[稲垣栄三|稲垣榮三]]・村瀬美樹・胡麻鶴醇之『神宮―第六十回神宮式年遷宮―』[[:en:小学館|小学館]]、昭和50th year 4月20日、246p. * 『日本人なら知っておきたい 伊勢神宮と125の社』タツミムック、[[辰巳出版]]、2013rd year 11月1日、127p. ISBN 978-4-7778-1197-7 == See Also == * {{ill|List of 125 Shrines of Ise|ja|神宮125社の一覧|lt=List of 125 Shrines of Ise|1=List of Shikinaisha in Ise Province|3=伊勢国の式内社一覧|1=List of Shikinaisha in Ise Province|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/List of 125 Shrines of Ise|13=User:Immanuelle/List of Shikinaisha in Ise Province|qq=}} * [[田丸町]]

'''{{ill|Sugihara Shrine|ja|棒原神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sugihara Shrine|qq=}}''' (*Sugihara Jinja*) is an auxiliary shrine (*sessha*) of {{ill|Kōtai Jingū|ja|皇大神宮|1=Kōtai Jingū|2=ja|3=皇大神宮|4=zh|5=皇大神宮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kōtai Jingū|13=User:Immanuelle/Kōtai Jingū|comment=enwiki is a redirect}} (the Inner Shrine of the [[Ise Grand Shrine]]), located in [[Tamaki Town]], {{ill|Mie Prefecture|en|Mie Prefecture|WD=Q128196|lt=Mie Prefecture}}, Japan. It Ranks 23rd among the auxiliary shrines of the Inner Shrine.<ref>Ujiyamada City Office (1929): pp. 9–11.</ref>
The shrine is dedicated to a deity associated with the {{ill|Pleiades|en|Pleiades|WD=Q13423|lt=Pleiades}} (*Subaru*), suggesting that ancient star-based divination practices were performed in this region.<ref name="ic">Ise Bunka Publishing (2008): p. 66.</ref><ref name="gk">Gakken Publishing (2013): p. 67.</ref>
== Overview == [[File:Sugihara-jinja 01.jpg|left|thumb|upright|Stone Steps Leading to the Shrine]]
Sugihara Shrine (杉原神社) is located in Asakuda 2466, Kamitanui, Tamaki Town, Watarai District, Mie Prefecture.<ref name="tm">Tatsumi Publishing (2013): p.57</ref> It is one of the 13 auxiliary shrines of the Inner Shrine in Tamaki Town. The shrine is situated atop a hill locally referred to as "Sugi no Mori" (Cedar Grove), with the shrine building located at the top of an 88-step stone staircase.<ref name="gk"/>
While the official reading of the shrine's name by Ise Grand Shrine is "Sugihara," some manuscripts of the "{{ill|Engishiki Jinmyōchō|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|ja|延喜式神名庫|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Engishiki Jinmyōchō|13=User:Immanuelle/Engishiki Jinmyōchō|qq=}}" read it as "Oihara."<ref name="sk8">Shikinaisha Kenkyukai (1990): p.358</ref>
The shrine precinct covers approximately 17,078 m², and the shrine building is constructed in the Shinmei-zukuri style with wooden plank roofing.<ref name="sk1">Shikinaisha Kenkyukai (1990): p.361</ref> The site includes a tamagaki (sacred fence) and a torii gate.<ref name="sk1"/> In ancient times, the shrine grounds spanned about 29,751 m², featuring two shrine buildings each measuring approximately 1.8 m x 1.2 m x 2.1 m.<ref name="sk8"/> The grounds also contain the "Asakuda Kofun Group," a collection of small Kofuns, including 10 round kofun and 14 square kofun.<ref name="sk1"/>
== Enshrined Deities == The enshrined deities are Amatsubaru-no-Mikoto-no-Mitama (天須婆留女命御魂) and Mimae-no-Kami (御前神).<ref name="ic"/><ref name="gk"/> They are considered guardian deities of the surrounding hills.<ref name="ic"/>
The name Amatsubaru-no-Mikoto-no-Mitama is derived from "Subaru" (Pleiades), and it is believed to have been associated with agricultural divination based on celestial observation.<ref name="ic"/> Hayakawa-hiko-no-Mikoto and Hayakawa-hime-no-Mikoto, enshrined at {{ill|Satakunari Shrine|ja|狭田国生神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Satakunari Shrine|qq=}}, are considered the children of Amatsubaru-no-Mikoto-no-Mitama.<ref name="th">Kaneko (1984): p.59</ref> Additionally, Nagakuchi-me-no-Mikoto, another child, is enshrined at {{ill|E Shrine|ja|狭田国生神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/E Shrine|qq=}}.<ref>Shikinaisha Kenkyukai (1990): p.306</ref> There is no physical sacred object representing the deity.<ref name="th"/>
== History == Sugihara Shrine is the newest among the auxiliary shrines in Tamaki Town, established during the Nara period after the Arakida clan developed the surrounding area.<ref>Kaneko (1984): p.69</ref> Initially, Mimae Shrine stood in front of Sugihara Shrine.<ref name="tm"/>
The shrine was abandoned during the Sengoku period but was restored on September 8, 1663 (Gregorian calendar: October 8, 1663) through the efforts of {{ill|Sakatekunari Shrine|ja|坂手国生神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sakatekunari Shrine|qq=}} and Shinto priests led by Kawabe Masanaga.<ref>Shikinaisha Kenkyukai (1990): p.277</ref> During its restoration, Mimae Shrine was merged into Sugihara Shrine.<ref name="tm"/> The restoration process was challenging as the exact location of the original shrine had been lost.<ref name="sk8 P359">Shikinaisha Kenkyukai (1990): p.359</ref>
The shrine was rebuilt in August 1918.<ref name="uy">Ujiyamada City Office (1929): p.11</ref>
== Rituals == Rituals are conducted by Shinto priests during major festivals, including the Three Seasonal Festivals (Tsukinamisai, Kannamesai), the Prayer Festival (Kinen-sai), the Harvest Festival (Niinamesai), and temporary offering festivals (Rinjiheihai-sai).<ref name="sk1"/>
== Surroundings == Rice paddies spread out to the south of the shrine grounds.<ref name="ic"/> On clear days, the Ise Sanzan (Three Mountains of Ise) can be seen to the west.<ref name="ic"/>
* **By Train:** Approximately 13 minutes on foot (about 715 m) from JR Sangu Line's Sotoda Station. * **By Car:** About 10 minutes (approximately 5.0 km) from the Tamaki Interchange on the Ise Expressway. == References ==

'''{{ill|Sugihara Shrine|ja|棒原神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sugihara Shrine|qq=}}''' (*Sugihara Jinja*) is an auxiliary shrine (*sessha*) of {{ill|Kōtai Jingū|ja|皇大神宮|1=Kōtai Jingū|2=ja|3=皇大神宮|4=zh|5=皇大神宮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kōtai Jingū|13=User:Immanuelle/Kōtai Jingū|comment=enwiki is a redirect}} (the Inner Shrine of the [[Ise Grand Shrine]]), located in [[Tamaki Town]], {{ill|Mie Prefecture|en|Mie Prefecture|WD=Q128196|lt=Mie Prefecture}}, Japan. It Ranks 23rd among the auxiliary shrines of the Inner Shrine.<ref>Ujiyamada City Office (1929): pp. 9–11.</ref>
The shrine is dedicated to a deity associated with the {{ill|Pleiades|en|Pleiades|WD=Q13423|lt=Pleiades}} (*Subaru*), suggesting that ancient star-based divination practices were performed in this region.<ref name="ic">Ise Bunka Publishing (2008): p. 66.</ref><ref name="gk">Gakken Publishing (2013): p. 67.</ref>
== Overview == [[File:Sugihara-jinja 01.jpg|left|thumb|upright|Stone Steps Leading to the Shrine]]
Sugihara Shrine (杉原神社) is located in Asakuda 2466, Kamitanui, Tamaki Town, Watarai District, Mie Prefecture.<ref name="tm">Tatsumi Publishing (2013): p.57</ref> It is one of the 13 auxiliary shrines of the Inner Shrine in Tamaki Town. The shrine is situated atop a hill locally referred to as "Sugi no Mori" (Cedar Grove), with the shrine building located at the top of an 88-step stone staircase.<ref name="gk"/>
While the official reading of the shrine's name by Ise Grand Shrine is "Sugihara," some manuscripts of the "{{ill|Engishiki Jinmyōchō|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|ja|延喜式神名庫|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Engishiki Jinmyōchō|13=User:Immanuelle/Engishiki Jinmyōchō|qq=}}" read it as "Oihara."<ref name="sk8">Shikinaisha Kenkyukai (1990): p.358</ref>
The shrine precinct covers approximately 17,078 m², and the shrine building is constructed in the Shinmei-zukuri style with wooden plank roofing.<ref name="sk1">Shikinaisha Kenkyukai (1990): p.361</ref> The site includes a tamagaki (sacred fence) and a torii gate.<ref name="sk1"/> In ancient times, the shrine grounds spanned about 29,751 m², featuring two shrine buildings each measuring approximately 1.8 m x 1.2 m x 2.1 m.<ref name="sk8"/> The grounds also contain the "Asakuda Kofun Group," a collection of small Kofuns, including 10 round kofun and 14 square kofun.<ref name="sk1"/>
== Enshrined Deities == The enshrined deities are Amatsubaru-no-Mikoto-no-Mitama (天須婆留女命御魂) and Mimae-no-Kami (御前神).<ref name="ic"/><ref name="gk"/> They are considered guardian deities of the surrounding hills.<ref name="ic"/>
The name Amatsubaru-no-Mikoto-no-Mitama is derived from "Subaru" (Pleiades), and it is believed to have been associated with agricultural divination based on celestial observation.<ref name="ic"/> Hayakawa-hiko-no-Mikoto and Hayakawa-hime-no-Mikoto, enshrined at {{ill|Satakunari Shrine|ja|狭田国生神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Satakunari Shrine|qq=}}, are considered the children of Amatsubaru-no-Mikoto-no-Mitama.<ref name="th">Kaneko (1984): p.59</ref> Additionally, Nagakuchi-me-no-Mikoto, another child, is enshrined at {{ill|E Shrine|ja|狭田国生神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/E Shrine|qq=}}.<ref>Shikinaisha Kenkyukai (1990): p.306</ref> There is no physical sacred object representing the deity.<ref name="th"/>
== History == Sugihara Shrine is the newest among the auxiliary shrines in Tamaki Town, established during the Nara period after the Arakida clan developed the surrounding area.<ref>Kaneko (1984): p.69</ref> Initially, Mimae Shrine stood in front of Sugihara Shrine.<ref name="tm"/>
The shrine was abandoned during the Sengoku period but was restored on September 8, 1663 (Gregorian calendar: October 8, 1663) through the efforts of {{ill|Sakatekunari Shrine|ja|坂手国生神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sakatekunari Shrine|qq=}} and Shinto priests led by Kawabe Masanaga.<ref>Shikinaisha Kenkyukai (1990): p.277</ref> During its restoration, Mimae Shrine was merged into Sugihara Shrine.<ref name="tm"/> The restoration process was challenging as the exact location of the original shrine had been lost.<ref name="sk8 P359">Shikinaisha Kenkyukai (1990): p.359</ref>
The shrine was rebuilt in August 1918.<ref name="uy">Ujiyamada City Office (1929): p.11</ref>
== Rituals == Rituals are conducted by Shinto priests during major festivals, including the Three Seasonal Festivals (Tsukinamisai, Kannamesai), the Prayer Festival (Kinen-sai), the Harvest Festival (Niinamesai), and temporary offering festivals (Rinjiheihai-sai).<ref name="sk1"/>
== Surroundings == Rice paddies spread out to the south of the shrine grounds.<ref name="ic"/> On clear days, the Ise Sanzan (Three Mountains of Ise) can be seen to the west.<ref name="ic"/>
* **By Train:** Approximately 13 minutes on foot (about 715 m) from JR Sangu Line's Sotoda Station. * **By Car:** About 10 minutes (approximately 5.0 km) from the Tamaki Interchange on the Ise Expressway. == References ==

ISBN 978-4-05-610047-1 * 金子延夫『玉城町史 第3巻―南伊勢の歴史と伝承―』三重県郷土資料叢書第92集、三重県郷土史料刊行会、昭和59th year 3月10日、308p. * 式内社研究会 編『式内社調査報告 第六巻 東海道1』{{ill|Kogakkan University|en|Kogakkan University|WD=Q1797584|lt=Kogakkan University}}出版部、平成2nd year 2月28日、690p. ISBN 978-4-7778-1197-7 == See Also == * {{ill|List of 125 Shrines of Ise|ja|神宮125社の一覧|lt=List of 125 Shrines of Ise|1=List of Shikinaisha in Ise Province|3=伊勢国の式内社一覧|1=List of Shikinaisha in Ise Province|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/List of 125 Shrines of Ise|13=User:Immanuelle/List of Shikinaisha in Ise Province|qq=}} * [[田丸町]]

The shrine is situated atop a hill locally referred to as "Sugi no Mori" (Cedar Grove), with the shrine building located at the top of an 88-step stone staircase.<ref name="gk"/>
While the official reading of the shrine's name by Ise Grand Shrine is "Sugihara," some manuscripts of the "{{ill|Engishiki Jinmyōchō|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|ja|延喜式神名庫|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Engishiki Jinmyōchō|13=User:Immanuelle/Engishiki Jinmyōchō|qq=}}" read it as "Oihara."<ref name="sk8">Shikinaisha Kenkyukai (1990): p.358</ref>
The shrine precinct covers approximately 17,078 m², and the shrine building is constructed in the Shinmei-zukuri style with wooden plank roofing.<ref name="sk1">Shikinaisha Kenkyukai (1990): p.361</ref> The site includes a tamagaki (sacred fence) and a torii gate.<ref name="sk1"/> In ancient times, the shrine grounds spanned about 29,751 m², featuring two shrine buildings each measuring approximately 1.8 m x 1.2 m x 2.1 m.<ref name="sk8"/> The grounds also contain the "Asakuda Kofun Group," a collection of small Kofuns, including 10 round kofun and 14 square kofun.<ref name="sk1"/>
== Enshrined Deities == The enshrined deities are Amatsubaru-no-Mikoto-no-Mitama (天須婆留女命御魂) and Mimae-no-Kami (御前神).<ref name="ic"/><ref name="gk"/> They are considered guardian deities of the surrounding hills.<ref name="ic"/>
The name Amatsubaru-no-Mikoto-no-Mitama is derived from "Subaru" (Pleiades), and it is believed to have been associated with agricultural divination based on celestial observation.<ref name="ic"/> Hayakawa-hiko-no-Mikoto and Hayakawa-hime-no-Mikoto, enshrined at {{ill|Satakunari Shrine|ja|狭田国生神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Satakunari Shrine|qq=}}, are considered the children of Amatsubaru-no-Mikoto-no-Mitama.<ref name="th">Kaneko (1984): p.59</ref> Additionally, Nagakuchi-me-no-Mikoto, another child, is enshrined at {{ill|E Shrine|ja|狭田国生神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/E Shrine|qq=}}.<ref>Shikinaisha Kenkyukai (1990): p.306</ref> There is no physical sacred object representing the deity.<ref name="th"/>
== History == Sugihara Shrine is the newest among the auxiliary shrines in Tamaki Town, established during the Nara period after the Arakida clan developed the surrounding area.<ref>Kaneko (1984): p.69</ref> Initially, Mimae Shrine stood in front of Sugihara Shrine.<ref name="tm"/>
The shrine was abandoned during the Sengoku period but was restored on September 8, 1663 (Gregorian calendar: October 8, 1663) through the efforts of {{ill|Sakatekunari Shrine|ja|坂手国生神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sakatekunari Shrine|qq=}} and Shinto priests led by Kawabe Masanaga.<ref>Shikinaisha Kenkyukai (1990): p.277</ref> During its restoration, Mimae Shrine was merged into Sugihara Shrine.<ref name="tm"/> The restoration process was challenging as the exact location of the original shrine had been lost.<ref name="sk8 P359">Shikinaisha Kenkyukai (1990): p.359</ref>
The shrine was rebuilt in August 1918.<ref name="uy">Ujiyamada City Office (1929): p.11</ref>
== Rituals == Rituals are conducted by Shinto priests during major festivals, including the Three Seasonal Festivals (Tsukinamisai, Kannamesai), the Prayer Festival (Kinen-sai), the Harvest Festival (Niinamesai), and temporary offering festivals (Rinjiheihai-sai).<ref name="sk1"/>
== Surroundings == Rice paddies spread out to the south of the shrine grounds.<ref name="ic"/> On clear days, the Ise Sanzan (Three Mountains of Ise) can be seen to the west.<ref name="ic"/>
* **By Train:** Approximately 13 minutes on foot (about 715 m) from JR Sangu Line's Sotoda Station. * **By Car:** About 10 minutes (approximately 5.0 km) from the Tamaki Interchange on the Ise Expressway. == References ==

The name Amatsubaru-no-Mikoto-no-Mitama is derived from "Subaru" (Pleiades), and it is believed to have been associated with agricultural divination based on celestial observation.<ref name="ic"/> Hayakawa-hiko-no-Mikoto and Hayakawa-hime-no-Mikoto, enshrined at {{ill|Satakunari Shrine|ja|狭田国生神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Satakunari Shrine|qq=}}, are considered the children of Amatsubaru-no-Mikoto-no-Mitama.<ref name="th">Kaneko (1984): p.59</ref> Additionally, Nagakuchi-me-no-Mikoto, another child, is enshrined at {{ill|E Shrine|ja|狭田国生神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/E Shrine|qq=}}.<ref>Shikinaisha Kenkyukai (1990): p.306</ref> There is no physical sacred object representing the deity.<ref name="th"/>
== History == Sugihara Shrine is the newest among the auxiliary shrines in Tamaki Town, established during the Nara period after the Arakida clan developed the surrounding area.<ref>Kaneko (1984): p.69</ref> Initially, Mimae Shrine stood in front of Sugihara Shrine.<ref name="tm"/>
The shrine was abandoned during the Sengoku period but was restored on September 8, 1663 (Gregorian calendar: October 8, 1663) through the efforts of {{ill|Sakatekunari Shrine|ja|坂手国生神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sakatekunari Shrine|qq=}} and Shinto priests led by Kawabe Masanaga.<ref>Shikinaisha Kenkyukai (1990): p.277</ref> During its restoration, Mimae Shrine was merged into Sugihara Shrine.<ref name="tm"/> The restoration process was challenging as the exact location of the original shrine had been lost.<ref name="sk8 P359">Shikinaisha Kenkyukai (1990): p.359</ref>
The shrine was rebuilt in August 1918.<ref name="uy">Ujiyamada City Office (1929): p.11</ref>
== Rituals == Rituals are conducted by Shinto priests during major festivals, including the Three Seasonal Festivals (Tsukinamisai, Kannamesai), the Prayer Festival (Kinen-sai), the Harvest Festival (Niinamesai), and temporary offering festivals (Rinjiheihai-sai).<ref name="sk1"/>
== Surroundings == Rice paddies spread out to the south of the shrine grounds.<ref name="ic"/> On clear days, the Ise Sanzan (Three Mountains of Ise) can be seen to the west.<ref name="ic"/>
* **By Train:** Approximately 13 minutes on foot (about 715 m) from JR Sangu Line's Sotoda Station. * **By Car:** About 10 minutes (approximately 5.0 km) from the Tamaki Interchange on the Ise Expressway. == References ==
